item.title: “For the most part, monks, beings find pleasure in sensual pleasures. But a clansman who has set aside the sickle and the carrying-pole, gone forth…
item.description:
“For the most part, monks, beings find pleasure in sensual pleasures. But a clansman who has set aside the sickle and the carrying-pole, gone forth from the household life into homelessness, is justifiably called ‘a clansman who has gone forth out of faith.’ Why is that? Sensual pleasures are found with youth, these sensual pleasures or others. And, monks, low sensual pleasures, middling sensual pleasures, and supreme sensual pleasures — all are classed as sensual pleasures.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.7
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Sunakkhatta, suppose there is a very poisonous viper. Then a man comes who desires to live, not to die, who desires happiness and loathes suffering.…
item.description:
“Sunakkhatta, suppose there is a very poisonous viper. Then a man comes who desires to live, not to die, who desires happiness and loathes suffering. What do you think, Sunakkhatta? Would that man give his hand or his foot to that viper if he knew: ‘If I am bitten by this snake, I will meet death or deadly suffering’?”
“No, venerable sir.”
“So too, Sunakkhatta, a monk should surely guard himself in regard to the six sense-bases. Knowing that ‘Attachment is the root of suffering,’ he becomes unattached and liberated by the destruction of attachment. It does not happen that he would still focus his body on attachment or direct his mind to attachment.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 105 – Sunakkhattasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Māṇava: “I saw an old person, I saw a sick person in pain, I saw a dead person, With life exhausted — Therefore I…
item.description:
Elder Māṇava:
“I saw an old person,
I saw a sick person in pain,
I saw a dead person,
With life exhausted —
Therefore I went forth,
Leaving the home life,
Abandoning sensual pleasures
That are enticing and agreeable.”

From: Theragāthā 1.73
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Thus, great king, a monk contemplates the five hindrances not yet abandoned as a debt, as a disease, as a prison, as slavery, as a…
item.description:
“Thus, great king, a monk contemplates the five hindrances not yet abandoned as a debt, as a disease, as a prison, as slavery, as a desert road. And, great king, just as being without debt, without disease, freed from prison, freed from slavery, in a safe and pleasant land — so, great king, a monk contemplates the five hindrances when they have been abandoned.”

From: Dīgha Nikāya 2 – Sāmaññaphalasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Good sirs, if a monk, reflecting, knows: ‘I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much with…
item.description:
“Good sirs, if a monk, reflecting, knows: ‘I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much with a mind overcome by sloth and torpor; I dwell much with restlessness; I dwell much with doubt; I dwell much with anger; I dwell much with a defiled mind; I dwell much with bodily fever; I dwell much with laziness; I dwell much with an unconcentrated mind’ — then that monk should make a strong wish, arouse effort, be energetic, strive, be strong-minded, not fall away, be mindful and clearly comprehending for the abandonment of those evil, unwholesome states. Just as, good sirs, if a turban or head were on fire, one would make a strong wish, arouse effort, be energetic, strive, be strong-minded, not fall away, be mindful and clearly comprehending to extinguish that turban or head — so too, that monk should make a strong wish … for the abandonment of those evil, unwholesome states. If a monk, reflecting, knows: ‘I dwell much with a mind free from greed; I dwell much with a mind free from hatred; I dwell much with a mind free from sloth and torpor; I dwell much with a mind free from restlessness; I dwell much with a mind that has overcome doubt; I dwell much with a mind free from anger; I dwell much with an undefiled mind; I dwell much with a body free from fever; I dwell much with an energetic, striving mind; I dwell much with a concentrated mind’ — then that monk, established in those wholesome states, should further devote himself to the destruction of the defilements.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 10.52
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Like a newly decorated medicine box, This foul body is adorned for the occasion — Enough to fool a fool, But not one who seeks…
item.description:
Like a newly decorated medicine box,
This foul body is adorned for the occasion —
Enough to fool a fool,
But not one who seeks the far shore.

The hunter set his trap and snare,
The wise deer does not approach,
Having eaten the crops, we go our way,
Leaving the trapper to lament.

From: Majjhima Nikāya 82 – Raṭṭhapālasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Venerable Udaya: “I have come with a question — To the meditator, sitting dustless, Duty done, without defilements, Who has reached the far shore —…
item.description:
Venerable Udaya:
“I have come with a question —
To the meditator, sitting dustless,
Duty done, without defilements,
Who has reached the far shore —
Speak the knowledge of liberation,
The breaking of ignorance.”

The Blessed One replied to Udaya:
“Abandon desire for sensual pleasures,
Both sorrow and grief;
Abandon sloth and torpor,
And prevent remorse.

Knowledge of liberation, I say,
Is the breaking of ignorance —
Purified by equanimity and mindfulness,
With reflection leading the way.”

Venerable Udaya:
“What binds the world?
What is its range?
By abandoning what is Nibbāna called?”

The Blessed One:
“The world is bound by delight;
Directed thought is its range.
By abandoning craving,
Nibbāna is called.”

From: Sutta Nipāta 5.14
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, here a monk, when there is sensual desire in him, knows: ‘There is sensual desire in me’; or when there is no sensual desire…
item.description:
“Monks, here a monk, when there is sensual desire in him, knows: ‘There is sensual desire in me’; or when there is no sensual desire in him, knows: ‘There is no sensual desire in me.’ And he knows how unarisen sensual desire arises; he knows how arisen sensual desire is abandoned; and he knows how abandoned sensual desire does not arise in the future.”

From: Dīgha Nikāya 22 – Mahāsatipaṭṭhānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And how, friend, does one guard the sense faculties? Here, friend, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he does not grasp the…
item.description:
“And how, friend, does one guard the sense faculties? Here, friend, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he does not grasp the general feature, does not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the eye faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise — he restrains that cause, guards the eye, practices restraint of the eye. When he hears a sound with the ear … smells an odor with the nose … tastes a flavor with the tongue … feels a touch with the body … cognizes a phenomenon with the mind, he does not grasp the general feature, does not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the mind faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise — he restrains that cause, guards the mind, practices restraint of the mind. Thus, friend, is the guarding of the sense faculties.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.120
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “This good teaching I give to you — You who have gathered here. Dig up the root of craving Like digging up birana grass. Do…
item.description:
“This good teaching I give to you —
You who have gathered here.
Dig up the root of craving
Like digging up birana grass.
Do not let Māra destroy you,
Like a flood destroying reeds.”

“Even though a tree is cut down,
If its root remains uncut,
The tree grows back —
So too, if the latent tendency to craving is not uprooted,
Suffering arises again and again.”

“The thirty-six streams of craving
Sweep away a person who is bent on pleasing thoughts —
Thoughts of lust,
Sweeping him away with wrong view.”

From: Dhammapada 334-359
📖 Read this sutta
